title: Flawed Merge Sort on Doubly Linked List

statement.markdown:
Given a doubly linked list where each node contains a value and a pointer to both the next node and the previous node. Implement a method that sorts the linked list in-place using a merge sort algorithm.

The merge sort algorithm should work as follows:

- Split the linked list into two halves
- Recursively apply the merge sort algorithm to each half
- Merge the two halves in-place

The linked list is represented as a node class with a value, prev pointer, and next pointer.

Example Input/Output:

Input: 1 -> 3 -> 5 -> 7 -> 9
Output: 1 -> 3 -> 5 -> 7 -> 9

The linked list should be sorted in ascending order without using any additional data structures.

Note: The input linked list may contain duplicate values.

Constraints:

- The linked list may be empty (i.e., head or tail may be null)
- The linked list may contain 1 to 10,000 nodes

HINT: When merging two halves, make sure to handle the case where the two halves have different lengths.
statement.inputFormat: Node* head; // head of the doubly linked list
statement.outputFormat: Node* head; // head of the sorted doubly linked list
statement.notes:
The merge sort algorithm should be implemented in-place, meaning that the original linked list should be modified to store the sorted result.
The time complexity of the merge sort algorithm should be O(n log n) where n is the number of nodes in the linked list.

examples.explanation: The linked list is already sorted in ascending order
The linked list is sorted in ascending order

hints.text: When merging two halves, make sure to handle the case where the two halves have different lengths.

editorial.title: Editorial - Flawed Merge Sort on Doubly Linked List
editorial.intuition:
The merge sort algorithm should be implemented in-place, meaning that the original linked list should be modified to store the sorted result.
The time complexity of the merge sort algorithm should be O(n log n) where n is the number of nodes in the linked list.

To implement the merge sort algorithm, we need to split the linked list into two halves, recursively apply the merge sort algorithm to each half, and then merge the two halves in-place.

When merging two halves, we need to handle the case where the two halves have different lengths.

The solution should be implemented in C++ and Python.
editorial.bruteForce:
The brute force solution would involve using a temporary array to store the elements of the linked list, sorting the array using the merge sort algorithm, and then reconstructing the linked list from the sorted array.

This solution would have a time complexity of O(n^2) and would not be efficient for large linked lists.

A more efficient solution would involve implementing the merge sort algorithm in-place, meaning that the original linked list should be modified to store the sorted result.

The time complexity of this solution would be O(n log n) and would be more efficient for large linked lists.

The solution should be implemented in C++ and Python.

editorial.proofOfCorrectness:

editorial.dryRun:
Let's take the linked list 1 -> 3 -> 5 -> 7 -> 9 and apply the merge sort algorithm to it.

First, we split the linked list into two halves: 1 -> 3 and 5 -> 7 -> 9.

Then, we apply the merge sort algorithm to each half.

For the first half, we split it into two halves: 1 and 3.

We apply the merge sort algorithm to each half and get the sorted half: 1 -> 3.

For the second half, we split it into two halves: 5 and 7 -> 9.

We apply the merge sort algorithm to each half and get the sorted half: 5 -> 7 -> 9.

Finally, we merge the two sorted halves in-place and get the sorted linked list: 1 -> 3 -> 5 -> 7 -> 9.

The solution handles the case where the two halves have different lengths by recursively splitting the linked list into two halves, applying the merge sort algorithm to each half, and then merging the two halves in-place.
